title: Advanced SQL
description: Go beyond basic queries. Learn how to combine data from multiple tables using JOINs, aggregate data for reporting, write complex subqueries, and use window functions for advanced analytical operations.

seo_metadata.title: Advanced SQL Certificate Course | ProCerix
seo_metadata.description: Master advanced SQL techniques. Learn JOINs, GROUP BY, subqueries, and window functions to solve complex data problems.

learning_outcomes: Combine data from multiple tables using INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL JOINs
Summarize data using aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT)
Group and filter aggregated data using GROUP BY and HAVING
Write complex subqueries and Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
Perform advanced analytics using Window Functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK)

modules.title: Combining Tables
modules.description: Bringing data together from across the database.

lessons.title: Understanding JOINs
lessons.content:
Relational Data

A JOIN clause is used to combine rows from two or more tables, based on a related column between them. The most common is the INNER JOIN, which returns records that have matching values in both tables.

lessons.title: Outer JOINs
lessons.content:
LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL

A LEFT JOIN returns all records from the left table, and the matched records from the right. If there is no match, the result is NULL on the right side. RIGHT JOIN does the opposite.

lessons.title: Aggregate Functions
lessons.content:
Math in SQL

Functions like COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MAX(), and MIN() perform a calculation on a set of values and return a single value.

lessons.title: GROUP BY and HAVING
lessons.content:
Grouping Results

The GROUP BY statement groups rows that have the same values into summary rows. The HAVING clause was added to SQL because the WHERE keyword cannot be used with aggregate functions.

modules.title: Advanced Query Techniques
modules.description: Complex logic and modern analytical functions.

lessons.title: Subqueries and CTEs
lessons.content:
Queries within Queries

A subquery is a query nested inside another statement. A Common Table Expression (CTE) using the WITH clause provides a more readable alternative to complex subqueries.

lessons.title: Window Functions
lessons.content:
Analytical Power

Window functions like ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and LEAD() perform a calculation across a set of table rows that are somehow related to the current row, without collapsing the rows like GROUP BY does.

mcqs.question: Which JOIN returns all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches in the right table?
Which SQL function is used to calculate the average value of a numeric column?
You cannot use a WHERE clause to filter based on an aggregate function. Which clause must you use instead?
What does a FULL OUTER JOIN do?

options.content: Returns only rows with matches in both tables
Returns all rows from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match
Deletes rows that do not match
Throws an error if tables are different sizes

mcqs.question: Which clause is used to define a Common Table Expression (CTE)?
A query nested inside a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement is called a:
Which of the following is a Window Function?
How does a Window Function differ from GROUP BY?

options.content: It only works on text data
It groups rows into a single output row
It performs a calculation across related rows but keeps the original rows intact

mcqs.question: If you want to count the number of rows in a table, which function do you use?
What is the typical order of clauses in a SQL statement?

options.content: SELECT, WHERE, FROM, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY
SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY
SELECT, FROM, GROUP BY, WHERE, ORDER BY, HAVING
FROM, SELECT, HAVING, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY
